project__lead_agency_information.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.cross_streets: Elkhorn Boulevard and Rio Linda Boulevard
project_location.within_2_miles_waterways: Dry Creek; Natomas East Main Drainage Canal
project_location.within_2_miles_airports: Sacramento International Airport (approx. 4.8 miles northwest, outside 2-mile radius); Rio Linda Airport (approx. 2.1 miles east, just outside 2-mile radius)
project_location.within_2_miles_railways: Union Pacific mainline (Sacramento Subdivision) approximately 1.4 miles east
project_location.within_2_miles_schools: Rio Linda High School; Orchard Elementary School; Westside Preparatory Charter School

development_type.transportation_type: Internal complete-street network with new signalized intersection at Elkhorn Blvd/Rio Linda Blvd, Class I multi-use trail connection to regional system, and new transit stop with bus pull-out and shelter.
development_type.other_development_type: Neighborhood-serving civic plaza with small community meeting room and public market pavilion (approx. 8,500 sq ft).

project_issues_discussed_in_document.other_project_issues: Energy consumption and conservation; greenhouse gas emissions and climate change resiliency; environmental justice considerations related to existing nearby disadvantaged communities; construction-period health risk from diesel particulate matter; and consistency with regional Sustainable Communities Strategy (SACOG MTP/SCS).

land_use__project_description.present_land_use_zoning_general_plan_des:
Existing Land Use: Predominantly fallow irrigated pasture with scattered outbuildings and one occupied single-family residence near the southwest corner; portions of the site are used intermittently for horse boarding and informal equipment storage.

Zoning: Primarily AG-20 (Agricultural, 20-acre minimum) with a small portion designated AR-5 (Agricultural-Residential, 5-acre minimum) near the existing residence.

General Plan Designation: Agricultural-Urban Reserve (County of Sacramento General Plan 2030) with a policy overlay identifying the area as within the Urban Services Boundary but outside the current Urban Policy Area, targeted for potential future urban development contingent on infrastructure availability and detailed planning.
land_use__project_description.project_description:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project proposes to transform an approximately 78.4-acre largely agricultural site at the northeast corner of Elkhorn Boulevard and Rio Linda Boulevard into a walkable, transit-supportive mixed-use neighborhood that provides a variety of housing types, neighborhood-serving commercial uses, employment opportunities, and public amenities.

In total, the project would develop up to 412 residential units, approximately 62,000 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial space, approximately 85,000 square feet of flexible office/employment space, and an 8,500-square-foot civic plaza and community meeting facility. Development would be clustered around a central "main street" spine that runs generally north–south from Elkhorn Boulevard into the interior of the site, with higher-intensity mixed-use buildings near the intersection and progressively lower-intensity residential neighborhoods toward the northern and eastern edges.

Residential uses would include a mix of small-lot single-family detached homes, duplexes, townhomes, and 3–4 story multifamily buildings. The preliminary program anticipates approximately 160 small-lot single-family units, 72 duplex and townhome units, and up to 180 multifamily units in two podium-style buildings above ground-floor parking and amenity space. At least 10 percent of the multifamily units (approximately 18 units) are proposed to be deed-restricted as affordable to lower-income households, targeted primarily to working families and seniors currently residing in the broader Rio Linda/Elkhorn corridor. The applicant has expressed an interest in partnering with a local nonprofit housing provider to manage the affordable units and provide on-site resident services such as financial counseling and after-school tutoring.

The commercial and office components would be concentrated near the Elkhorn Boulevard/Rio Linda Boulevard intersection, with building forms that frame the corner and create an identifiable neighborhood “gateway.” Neighborhood-serving retail uses may include a small-format grocery store, café/coffee shop, pharmacy, sit-down and quick-service restaurants, and personal services such as a barber shop, dry cleaner, and fitness studio. The office buildings are anticipated to accommodate a mix of medical and dental offices, professional services (e.g., small engineering or architecture firms), co-working space for remote workers, and potentially a satellite office for a public agency or community-based organization. The project applicant has indicated that at least one of the office buildings will be designed with flexible floor plates to accommodate either traditional office users or light R&D/innovation tenants, depending on market demand at the time of construction.

A centrally located civic plaza (approximately 0.75 acres) would provide a focal point for community activity and would be framed by ground-floor commercial uses and upper-story residential units. The plaza would include shade trees, flexible event space, outdoor seating, and a small, single-story community meeting room and public market pavilion (approximately 8,500 square feet combined) that could host farmers’ markets, neighborhood association meetings, cultural events, and youth programming. The applicant has discussed, in early community outreach meetings, a desire to host monthly multicultural night markets that highlight local food vendors, artisans, and performing artists from the surrounding communities of Rio Linda and North Sacramento.

The project proposes an interconnected internal circulation network designed according to "complete streets" principles. Streets would include continuous sidewalks, Class II bike lanes on primary internal roadways, and traffic-calming features such as curb extensions, raised crosswalks, and street trees. A Class I multi-use trail would run along the eastern edge of the site, paralleling an existing irrigation canal, and would connect to the broader regional trail network, including planned links to the Dry Creek Parkway. The project would construct a new signalized intersection at Elkhorn Boulevard and the project’s main entrance, including dedicated turn lanes, pedestrian countdown signals, high-visibility crosswalks, and bus stop improvements (bus pull-outs, shelters, benches, and real-time transit information signage) to support existing and future Sacramento Regional Transit bus routes.

Approximately 12.3 acres of the site would be devoted to parks, open space, and recreational amenities. These include two neighborhood parks (approximately 3.5 and 2.8 acres respectively) with playgrounds, multi-use turf fields, picnic areas, and half-court basketball facilities; several smaller pocket parks and paseos interwoven into the residential neighborhoods; and a linear greenway along the multi-use trail corridor with native landscaping, interpretive signage regarding local ecology and agricultural history, and informal seating areas. The project also includes a small dog park and community garden plots, which were specifically requested during early outreach meetings with nearby residents who expressed a desire for more opportunities for intergenerational interaction and access to fresh produce.

Infrastructure improvements would include extension and upsizing of public water, sewer, and storm drainage facilities to serve the site and provide capacity for anticipated future development in the immediate vicinity. Domestic water would be provided by the Sacramento County Water Agency via connection to existing mains in Elkhorn Boulevard, with on-site looped distribution lines and individual service connections to each building. Sanitary sewer flows would be collected via a gravity system and conveyed to the existing regional interceptor system; preliminary hydraulic modeling indicates that downstream capacity improvements may be required at two existing manholes, and the project applicant has committed to funding its fair share of those improvements. Stormwater management would rely on a combination of low-impact development (LID) features—such as bioretention planters, permeable pavement in selected parking areas, and vegetated swales—and two on-site detention basins located near the northern boundary of the site. These basins would be designed to meet Sacramento County’s stormwater quality treatment and flood control requirements and would be landscaped to provide habitat value and visual amenity.

The project would be developed in three primary phases over an anticipated 8- to 10-year buildout period, depending on market conditions. Phase 1 would focus on backbone infrastructure, the initial mixed-use buildings at the Elkhorn/Rio Linda intersection, the civic plaza, and approximately 150–180 residential units. Phase 2 would extend the internal street network to the north and east, deliver the first neighborhood park and additional residential units, and construct one of the two office buildings. Phase 3 would complete the remaining residential neighborhoods, the second neighborhood park, and any remaining commercial/office space. Construction activities would generally occur between 7:00 a.m. and 7:00 p.m. on weekdays, with limited Saturday work as needed, in accordance with local noise ordinances. The applicant has indicated an intent to implement a construction management plan that addresses truck routing, dust control, noise minimization, and communication with nearby residents, including a dedicated project website, periodic email updates, and a hotline for complaints or questions.

From a policy standpoint, the project requires several discretionary approvals from the City and coordination with Sacramento County and regional agencies. These include: (1) a General Plan Amendment to redesignate the site from Agricultural-Urban Reserve to a combination of Mixed Use Neighborhood and Low Density Residential, with a parks/open space overlay; (2) a Specific Plan (Riverbend Village Specific Plan) establishing land use designations, development standards, design guidelines, and phasing requirements; (3) a Planned Unit Development (PUD) to allow for flexible lotting patterns, mixed-use building forms, and tailored development standards (e.g., reduced setbacks and parking ratios in the core mixed-use area) in exchange for enhanced amenities and design quality; (4) a Rezone from AG-20 and AR-5 to a mix of MU-V (Mixed Use Village), RD-10, and RD-20 districts; (5) a Tentative Subdivision Map to create individual residential lots, commercial/office parcels, public street rights-of-way, and park/open space lots; (6) a Major Site Plan and Design Review to ensure consistency with the City’s design guidelines; (7) one or more Conditional Use Permits for specific uses such as a drive-through restaurant or tasting room, if pursued by future tenants; and (8) approval of a Development Agreement establishing vested rights and specifying the timing and funding of infrastructure and community benefit obligations.

The project applicant, Riverbend Community Partners, LLC—a locally based development entity whose principal, Daniel Alvarez, grew up in the Rio Linda area—has emphasized in community meetings that one of the project’s primary goals is to provide housing and amenities that serve existing residents rather than displace them. For example, Mr. Alvarez has shared stories of his parents driving 20–25 minutes into central Sacramento for basic services like banking, groceries, or a pediatrician visit, and he has expressed a desire to bring those services closer to where people already live. The applicant has also described an intention to work with local workforce development organizations to prioritize construction and permanent jobs for residents of nearby neighborhoods, including youth apprenticeship programs and targeted outreach to underrepresented groups in the building trades.

In sum, the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is intended to create a compact, mixed-use, and amenity-rich neighborhood that supports regional smart growth objectives, provides a range of housing opportunities, and delivers meaningful public benefits—including parks, trails, and improved transit access—while carefully managing and mitigating potential environmental impacts identified through the CEQA process.
